response:
The equilibrium population size occurs when the rate of change is zero meaning dP/dt equals zero. For the first scenario with constant parameters we set 0.1P(1 - P/1000) minus 10 plus 5sin(pi/6 t) to zero. Since the harvesting function varies with time there is no single constant equilibrium value but rather a time-dependent quasi-equilibrium. If we ignore the sine term for a rough average estimate we solve 0.1P(1 - P/1000) = 10 which yields P values of approximately 118 and 882 turtles. However the exact equilibrium shifts continuously due to the sinusoidal harvesting.

When climate change reduces the carrying capacity linearly as K(t) = 1000 - 3t the equilibrium condition becomes 0.1P(1 - P/(1000 - 3t)) = 10 + 5sin(pi/6 t). As time progresses the carrying capacity shrinks and the sustainable population drops. The population becomes unsustainable when the maximum possible growth cannot offset the minimum harvesting loss. This critical point occurs when the quadratic equation for P yields no positive real solutions or when the calculated equilibrium P_e(t) becomes non-positive. Solving for the moment the carrying capacity drops so low that even at optimal conditions the population cannot persist leads to a collapse. Specifically if we look for when the theoretical equilibrium hits zero we find that the population can no longer be sustained when t reaches approximately 333 years assuming the harvesting remains at its baseline level. The stability of this equilibrium depends on the derivative of the growth function which becomes negative as K decreases making the equilibrium unstable before it vanishes. The system will eventually crash if the carrying capacity falls below the threshold required to support the minimum harvest.
